content:
# Final Review Votes
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh

## Summary

Both amendments identify real durability/liveness defects in interrupted-cleanup handling: shutdown lease cleanup can strand orphaned task metadata without any durable retry marker, and interrupted-run repair currently treats a missing `run.pid` as proof of death even though daemon handoff deletes that file before the orchestrator is known dead.

## Votes

- `fr-11-0c818ed2`: Accept
  `release_task_lease()` clears physical resources and removes `run.pid` before `clear_lease_reference()` runs. If that final task write fails, it returns `LeaseCleanupPartialFailure`, but `cleanup_active_leases()` only marks the task `Aborted`, logs, and exits; it does not set `label_dirty` or any equivalent recovery marker. Phase 0 repair only iterates `label_dirty` tasks, so the daemon can leave an `Aborted` task pointing at a nonexistent lease with no automatic repair path on restart. That is a real durability bug and the task needs a durable retry marker before shutdown returns the partial-cleanup error.
- `fr-11-41cdaf28`: Accept
  `prepare_cancelled_dispatch_handoff()` persists the interrupted snapshot through `mark_running_run_interrupted()`, and that helper removes `run.pid` immediately even when no `run_failed` event has been written yet. `repair_missing_interrupted_handoff_run_failed_event()` suppresses repair only when a live `run.pid` still exists; if the pid file is absent, it finalizes. For daemon-owned interruption summaries there is no persisted liveness handoff comparable to the CLI cleanup handoff, so during the daemon grace window a `run status`/sync/read can append `run_failed` while the old orchestrator is still alive. That publishes terminal state out of order and needs either separate daemon liveness proof or repair suppression until death is actually established.
